symbols.name: Oxford 3000
symbols.description: in the Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary , shows a word from the Oxford 3000 with its CEFR level . Click on this icon to see a list of other words that are part of the Oxford 3000 at the same CEFR level.

symbols.name: Oxford 5000
symbols.description: in the Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary , shows a word from the Oxford 5000 with its CEFR level . Click on this icon to see a list of other words that are part of the Oxford 5000 at the same CEFR level.

symbols.name: Oxford 3000
symbols.description: shows a word from the Oxford 3000 . Click on this icon to see a list of other words that are part of the Oxford 3000.

symbols.name: CEFR
symbols.description: shows the CEFR level of a word or phrase

symbols.name: OPAL written
symbols.description: in the Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary , shows a word from the OPAL list of written words. Click on this icon to see the full OPAL sublist.

symbols.name: OPAL written
symbols.description: in the Oxford Learner's Dictionary of Academic English , shows a word from the OPAL word list of written words. Click on this icon to see a list of all the words on the same OPAL sublist.

symbols.name: OPAL spoken
symbols.description: in the Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary , shows a word from the OPAL list of spoken words. Click on this icon to see the full OPAL sublist.

symbols.name: OPAL spoken
symbols.description: in the Oxford Learner's Dictionary of Academic English , shows a word from the OPAL word list of spoken words. Click on this icon to see a list of other words on the same OPAL sublist.

symbols.name: Academic Word List
symbols.description: shows a word from the Academic Word List . Click on this icon to see a list of other words that are part of the Academic Word List.

symbols.name: My Wordlists
symbols.description: adds a word, phrasal verb or idiom to My Word Lists

symbols.name: derivative symbol
symbols.description: shows a derivative. Derivatives can be easily understood from the meaning of the word from which they are derived (the headword).

symbols.name: phrasal verb arrow
symbols.description: in phrasal verbs, shows that the object may come either before or after the particle

symbols.name: cross reference arrow
symbols.description: shows a cross reference to another related entry in the dictionary

symbols.name: external link icon
symbols.description: shows a cross reference to the corresponding entry in another dictionary

register_labels.name: approving
register_labels.description: expressions show that you feel approval or admiration, for example feisty , petite .

register_labels.name: disapproving
register_labels.description: expressions show that you feel disapproval or contempt, for example blinkered , newfangled .

register_labels.name: figurative
register_labels.description: language is used in a non-literal or metaphorical way, as in He didn't want to cast a shadow on (= spoil) their happiness.
examples_given: He didn't want to cast a shadow on (= spoil) their happiness

register_labels.name: formal
register_labels.description:
expressions are usually only used in serious or official language and would not be appropriate in normal everyday conversation. Examples are admonish , besmirch .
        (OLDAE users: note that academic writing is generally fairly formal in tone, as are many of the words in this dictionary. The formal label is used in OLDAE only for those words that are formal even by the standards of academic writing.)

register_labels.name: humorous
register_labels.description: expressions are intended to be funny, for example fisticuffs , ignoramus .

register_labels.name: informal
register_labels.description: expressions are used between friends or in a relaxed or unofficial situation. They are not appropriate for formal situations. Examples are bonkers , dodgy . (OLDAE users: note that informal expressions are not usually appropriate in academic writing, so very few are included in this dictionary. However, OLDAE includes a number of rather informal expressions which may be used in more informal types of academic writing.)

register_labels.name: ironic
register_labels.description: language uses words to mean the opposite of the meaning that they seem to have, as in You're a great help, I must say! (= no help at all).
examples_given: You're a great help, I must say! (= no help at all)

register_labels.name: literary
register_labels.description: language is used mainly in literature and imaginative writing, for example aflame , halcyon .

register_labels.name: offensive
register_labels.description: expressions are used by some people to address or refer to people in a way that is very insulting, especially in connection with their race, religion, sex or disabilities. You should not use these words.

register_labels.name: slang
register_labels.description: is very informal language, sometimes restricted to a particular group of people, for example people of the same age or those who have the same interests or do the same job. Examples are dosh , gnarly .

register_labels.name: specialist
register_labels.description: language is used by people who specialize in particular subject areas, for example accretion , adipose .

register_labels.name: taboo
register_labels.description: expressions are likely to be thought by many people to be obscene or shocking. You should not use them.

usage_restrictions.name: dialect
usage_restrictions.description: describes expressions that are mainly used in particular regions of the British Isles, not including Ireland, Scotland or Wales, for example beck , nowt .

usage_restrictions.name: old-fashioned
usage_restrictions.description:
expressions are passing out of current use, for example beanfest , bothersome .
        (OLDAE users: note that old-fashioned expressions may be appropriate when writing about historical contexts.)

usage_restrictions.name: old use
usage_restrictions.description: describes expressions that are no longer in current use, for example ere , perchance .

usage_restrictions.name: saying
usage_restrictions.description: describes a well-known fixed or traditional phrase, such as a proverb, that is used to make a comment, give advice, etc., for example actions speak louder than words .
shows a trademark of a manufacturing company, for example Band-Aid , Frisbee .
